title: The silence between steps
summary: A young tourist learns tango from an old teacher in Buenos Aires. She asks why they stop in the middle of the music.

body.speaker: Emma
body.text: One, two, three… and step. Like this?

body.speaker: Doña Rosa
body.text: Yes, child. Again.

body.speaker: Emma
body.text: One, two, three… One, two… stop.

body.speaker: Doña Rosa
body.text: Good. Stop in the same place.

body.speaker: Emma
body.text: Doña Rosa, why do we stop here? The music does not stop. Why do we stop?

body.speaker: Doña Rosa
body.text: Listen. Not to the music. To us. We stop because we need to listen.

body.speaker: Emma
body.text: To what?

body.speaker: Doña Rosa
body.text: To the breath. To the floor. To the next foot.

body.speaker: Emma
body.text: I do not understand.

body.speaker: Doña Rosa
body.text: Tango is not the steps. Tango is the silence between the steps. The tourists from your country want all steps and no silence. They are tired. They are angry. They go home. They learn nothing.

body.speaker: Emma
body.text: Show me again.

body.speaker: Doña Rosa
body.text: One, two, three… One, two, three… Stop.

body.speaker: Emma
body.text: How long is the stop?

body.speaker: Doña Rosa
body.text: Two breaths. Sometimes one. Once my husband stopped for five. The room was quiet. The music was loud. We stood still. Then we danced again.

body.speaker: Emma
body.text: Your husband danced with you?

body.speaker: Doña Rosa
body.text: Forty-one years. He died in 2019. I dance with my students now. They are my partners.

body.speaker: Emma
body.text: Doña Rosa, I am sorry.

body.speaker: Doña Rosa
body.text: Do not be sorry. Stop. Listen. Then dance.

body.speaker: Emma
body.text: One, two, three… One, two, three… stop.

body.speaker: Doña Rosa
body.text: Yes. The silence. That is your tango.

annotations.meaningVi: đứng yên không động đậy (cụm động từ cố định)
chỉ cho tôi xem lại (yêu cầu lặp lại minh họa)
nhảy lại / tiếp tục nhảy (again = thêm một lần nữa)
đừng xin lỗi / đừng thương hại (cấu trúc mệnh lệnh phủ định: do not + be + adj)
không học được gì (nothing = không có gì, nhấn mạnh hơn 'not anything')
bao lâu (hỏi về khoảng thời gian)
tôi không hiểu (thành ngữ giao tiếp thường dùng khi học tiếng Anh)
yên lặng / im ắng (quiet = không ồn ào; khác với 'silent' = hoàn toàn không có tiếng)
giữa các bước (giới từ 'between' chỉ vị trí ở khoảng giữa hai thứ hoặc nhiều đơn vị rời rạc)

grammarNotes.title: Short imperatives
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Để ra lệnh, hướng dẫn hoặc khuyên ngắn gọn, A1 dùng câu mệnh lệnh — bắt đầu bằng động từ nguyên thể, không cần chủ ngữ. 'Stop.' 'Listen.' 'Then dance.' 'Show me again.' Câu phủ định: 'Do not + động từ': 'Do not be sorry.' Câu mệnh lệnh thường rất ngắn và rõ — đúng nhịp dạy nhảy.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Imperatives are short commands or instructions. Just use the base verb, with no subject: 'Stop.' 'Listen.' 'Then dance.' 'Show me again.' The negative is 'Do not + base verb': 'Do not be sorry.' Imperatives are short and direct — they match the rhythm of teaching.

translationVi:
Emma: Một, hai, ba… rồi bước. Vậy này phải không ạ?
Doña Rosa: Đúng rồi, con. Lại lần nữa.
Emma: Một, hai, ba… một, hai… dừng.
Doña Rosa: Tốt. Dừng đúng chỗ đó.
Emma: Doña Rosa, sao mình dừng ở đây? Nhạc đâu có dừng. Sao mình dừng?
Doña Rosa: Lắng nghe. Không phải nhạc. Lắng nghe mình. Mình dừng vì mình cần lắng nghe.
Emma: Nghe cái gì ạ?
Doña Rosa: Hơi thở. Nền nhà. Bước chân tiếp theo.
Emma: Con không hiểu.
Doña Rosa: Tango không phải là các bước. Tango là khoảng im lặng giữa các bước. Khách du lịch nước con muốn toàn bước, không có im lặng. Họ mệt. Họ bực. Họ về nhà. Họ không học được gì.
Emma: Bà dạy lại con đi.
Doña Rosa: Một, hai, ba… một, hai, ba… Dừng.
Emma: Dừng bao lâu ạ?
Doña Rosa: Hai hơi thở. Có khi một. Một lần chồng bà dừng năm hơi. Phòng im. Nhạc to. Hai vợ chồng đứng yên. Rồi nhảy tiếp.
Emma: Chồng bà từng nhảy với bà ạ?
Doña Rosa: Bốn mươi mốt năm. Ông mất năm 2019. Giờ bà nhảy với học trò. Học trò là bạn nhảy của bà.
Emma: Doña Rosa, con xin lỗi.
Doña Rosa: Đừng xin lỗi. Dừng. Lắng nghe. Rồi nhảy.
Emma: Một, hai, ba… một, hai, ba… dừng.
Doña Rosa: Đúng rồi. Khoảng im lặng. Đó là tango của con.

questions.explanation: Doña Rosa speaks about 'tourists from your country' and calls her 'child' as a student.

questions.prompt: Why does Doña Rosa tell Emma to stop in the middle of the music?
options: Because Emma is doing the steps wrong.
Because the dance is the silence between the steps, and they need to listen.
questions.explanation: 'Tango is the silence between the steps.'
questions.hint: Doña Rosa's longer speech about tango.

questions.prompt: What three things does Doña Rosa say they listen to?
options: The breath, the floor, and the next foot
The music, the door, and the window
The radio, the clock, and the street
The teacher, the band, and the audience
questions.explanation: 'To the breath. To the floor. To the next foot.'
questions.hint: Three short sentences in a row.

questions.prompt: What does Doña Rosa say about tourists who want only steps and no silence?
options: They are tired and angry, they go home, and they learn nothing.
questions.explanation: 'They are tired. They are angry. They go home. They learn nothing.'
questions.hint: End of Doña Rosa's longer speech.

questions.prompt: How long can the silence be?
options: Two breaths, sometimes one — once her husband stopped for five.
questions.explanation: 'Two breaths. Sometimes one. Once my husband stopped for five.'

options: Her dance partner for forty-one years
questions.explanation: 'Forty-one years. He died in 2019.' Earlier she calls students her 'partners.'
questions.hint: Doña Rosa replies when Emma asks about her husband.

questions.prompt: Who does Doña Rosa dance with now?
options: Her students — they are her partners now.
questions.explanation: 'I dance with my students now. They are my partners.'
questions.hint: Look right after Doña Rosa talks about her husband.

questions.prompt: What does Doña Rosa say when Emma says 'I am sorry'?
options: 'Do not be sorry. Stop. Listen. Then dance.'
questions.explanation: Doña Rosa refuses pity and gives a short lesson instead.
questions.hint: Three short commands in a row.

questions.prompt: What does Doña Rosa mean by 'That is your tango' at the end?
options: Emma should buy a tango book.
Emma has finally found the silence — that is what tango really is.
Emma should perform in public tomorrow.
questions.explanation: Emma stopped at the right place; Doña Rosa says the silence is the dance.
questions.hint: Look at what Emma did right before — 'one, two, three… stop.'

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the dialogue?
options: The most important part of a thing is often the small still place inside it.
Buenos Aires is the only city for tango.
questions.explanation: Doña Rosa teaches that the silence between steps is the tango.
questions.hint: Think about what Doña Rosa keeps coming back to.

cloze.template:
Emma: One, two, three… and step. Like this?
Doña Rosa: Yes, child. Again.
Emma: One, two, three… One, two… {{b1}}.
Doña Rosa: Good. Stop in the same place.
Emma: Doña Rosa, why do we stop here? The {{b2}} does not stop. Why do we stop?
Doña Rosa: {{b3}}. Not to the music. To us. We stop because we need to listen.
Emma: To what?
Doña Rosa: To the breath. To the floor. To the next {{b4}}.
Emma: I do not understand.
Doña Rosa: Tango is not the steps. Tango is the {{b5}} between the steps. The tourists from your country want all steps and no silence. They are tired. They are angry. They go home. They learn nothing.
Emma: Show me again.
Doña Rosa: One, two, three… One, two, three… Stop.
Emma: How long is the stop?
Doña Rosa: Two {{b6}}. Sometimes one. Once my husband stopped for five. The room was quiet. The music was loud. We stood still. Then we danced again.
Emma: Your husband danced with you?
Doña Rosa: Forty-{{b7}} years. He died in 2019. I dance with my students now. They are my {{b8}}.
Emma: Doña Rosa, I am sorry.
Doña Rosa: Do not be {{b9}}. Stop. Listen. Then dance.
Emma: One, two, three… One, two, three… stop.
Doña Rosa: Yes. The silence. That is your {{b10}}.

blanks.explanation: Doña Rosa tells her to listen.
Tango is the silence between the steps.
Her students are her partners now.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Doña Rosa says tango is the silence between the steps, not the steps. Is there something you 'do' — talk, work, run — where the part you skip is actually the part that matters? What changes if you stop where everyone expects you to keep going?
